elements.sent-2411-001: Determining potential probability distributions with a given causal graph is vital for causality studies.
elements.sent-2411-002: To bypass the difficulty in characterizing latent variables in a Bayesian network, the nested Markov model provides an elegant algebraic approach by listing exactly all the equality constraints on the observed variables.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, this algebraically motivated causal model comprises distributions outside Bayesian networks, and its physical interpretation remains vague.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we inspect the nested Markov model through the lens of generalized probabilistic theory, an axiomatic framework to describe general physical theories.
elements.sent-2411-005: We prove that all the equality constraints defining the nested Markov model hold valid theory-independently.
elements.sent-2411-006: Yet, we show this model generally contains distributions not implementable even within such relaxed physical theories subjected to merely the relativity principles and mild probabilistic rules.
elements.sent-2411-007: To interpret the origin of such a gap, we establish a new causal model that defines valid distributions as projected from a high-dimensional Bell-type causal structure.
elements.sent-2411-008: The new model unveils inequality constraints induced by relativity principles, or equivalently high-dimensional conditional independences, which are absent in the nested Markov model.
elements.sent-2411-009: Nevertheless, we also notice that the restrictions on states and measurements introduced by the generalized probabilistic theory framework can pose additional inequality constraints beyond the new causal model.
elements.sent-2411-010: As a by-product, we discover a new causal structure exhibiting strict gaps between the distribution sets of a Bayesian network, generalized probabilistic theories, and the nested Markov model.
elements.sent-2411-011: We anticipate our results will enlighten further explorations on the unification of algebraic and physical perspectives of causality.
elements.sent-2411-012: Understanding the relationship between various events is the everlasting theme of applied sciences.
elements.sent-2411-013: In this quest, the concept of independence is central to our reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-014: Mathematically, independence is formalized via probabilities, where the occurrence probability of an event is not affected by another.
elements.sent-2411-015: Based on this, we define correlation between variables as the opposite notion.
elements.sent-2411-016: To interpret the origin of correlations, a natural thought suggests two causal explanations: one event might have a direct causal effect on the other, and it is also possible to have unobserved issues influencing the correlated events in common.
elements.sent-2411-017: With continuous efforts to formulate these notions as rigorous mathematical expressions, we embrace the modern framework of causality in terms of algebra~\cite{pearl2009causality .}
elements.sent-2411-018: From a physical perspective, independence and causal effects are firmly grounded.
elements.sent-2411-019: An observer's information acquisition is reflected in the observation of events~\cite{brukner2017quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-020: For a variable to directly influence other variables, it needs to transmit information to the latter.
elements.sent-2411-021: According to the principles of relativity theory, information transmission is subjected to the arrow of time and fundamentally restricted by the light speed in the underlying spacetime~\cite{einstein1922general .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Consequently, these physical notions justify the use of directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) for the study of causal influences~\cite{pearl1987logic,pearl1995causal .}
elements.sent-2411-023: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Bell , a variable is denoted as a vertex, and a direct causal influence is denoted as a directed edge.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Given a causal structure depicted by a DAG, what is the complete set of allowed correlations among observable variables? Aiming at this question, the nested Markov model provides an algebraic approach~\cite{tian2002testable,evans2017margins,richardson2023nested .}
elements.sent-2411-025: The nested Markov model delineates a statistical manifold with equality constraints over observable correlations.
elements.sent-2411-026: Specifically, these constraints include the usual conditional independences between random variables, which are graphically specified as the d-separation~\cite{geiger1990logic,verma1990causal,geiger1990separation , and nonlinear constraints termed Verma's constraints~\cite{robins1986new,verma1990equivalence .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The nested Markov model is algebraically complete, and as proven recently, the equality constraints coincide with those in a Bayesian network with latent variables~\cite{evans2017margins , where all the variables, including both the observed and unobserved ones, are entirely modelled as random variables.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In other words, the nested Markov model and the Bayesian network are the same in terms of equality constraints of the observed variables, and differ only in inequality constraints~\cite{evans2017margins .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Despite these appealing algebraic properties, the physical meaning of nested Markov model is largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-030: To our current physical understanding, observed events may originate from quantum measurements~\cite{sakurai2020modern .}
elements.sent-2411-031: As shown by the violation of Bell inequalities, correlations beyond the reach of Bayesian networks can be generated when the latent issues admit a quantum nature~\cite{bell1964einstein,clauser1969proposed .}
elements.sent-2411-032: In exploring other plausible physical theories in accordance with relativity, generalized probabilistic theories (GPTs) have been proposed~\cite{barrett2007information,chiribella2010probabilistic,janotta2014generalized , which may support even stronger correlations between nonlocal observed variables~\cite{popescu1994quantum,barrett2007information .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: It remains vague whether the nested Markov model fully complies with the notion of independence in general physics.
elements.sent-2411-034: Moreover, by definition, the nested Markov model does not explicitly pose inequality constraints to correlations.
elements.sent-2411-035: On the other hand, as reflected by the differences in the Bell inequality violations in various physical theories, the physical modelling of causality contact restricts the strength of correlations~\cite{acin2016certified .}
elements.sent-2411-036: It is unclear if the nested Markov model is too ``loose,'' comprising ``non-physical'' correlations.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this work, we examine the physical interpretation of nested Markov models.
elements.sent-2411-038: We first prove that the equality constraints in the nested Markov model remain sound and complete for GPTs, namely, theory-independent.
elements.sent-2411-039: On the other hand, we also confirm the existence of causal structures where GPTs unavoidably pose additional inequality constraints.
elements.sent-2411-040: To better understand the gap between the nested Markov model and GPTs, we construct a new causality model standing between them.
elements.sent-2411-041: Roughly speaking, the set of correlations in the new model is defined as a specific projection result from the nested Markov model of an associated Bell-type hyper-graph.
elements.sent-2411-042: Using the new causal model, we explicitly demonstrate that the set of probability distributions encoded by GPTs contains two types of inequality constraints: one type emerges from the projection of independence conditions in the hyper graph, and the other originates from the GPT states and measurements.
elements.sent-2411-043: In other words, there are also causal structures where a strict gap exists between this projection-based and GPT causal theories.
elements.sent-2411-044: We shall provide concrete examples on this.
elements.sent-2411-045: As we will elaborate later, we suggest that the new causal model extended by the projection procedure supersets any physically motivated causal theory.
elements.sent-2411-046: The projection technique in constructing the new causal model allows us to borrow mature techniques in the field of Bell nonlocality to causal studies.
elements.sent-2411-047: As a by-product, by applying the projection to the multipartite nonlocal correlation in the ``guess-your-neighbour's-input (GYNI)'' Bell test~\cite{almeida2010guess , we discover a new causal structure with a strict separation between the set of valid correlations in classical physics, GPTs and nested Markov models.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Before we commence, we first review the basic concepts of GPTs and causality.
elements.sent-2411-049: Starting from the investigation into the interpretation of quantum physics, the GPT framework was proposed to study and compare various physical theories~\cite{barrett2007information .}
elements.sent-2411-050: As the name suggests, an underlying assumption of GPTs is the validity of probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-051: In statistical theory, we can abstract the essential features of probabilities and formalize the concept of a probability kernel.
elements.sent-2411-052: Following~\cite{lauritzengraphical , given finite state spaces, $\mathcal{X _A,\mathcal{X _B$, a probability kernel over $\mathcal{X _A$ given $\mathcal{X _B$ is a non-negative function \begin{equation}\label{def:kernel} q:\mathcal{X}_A\times\mathcal{X}_B\rightarrow\mathbb{R}_{+},\end{equation} such that $\forall x_B\in\mathcal{X _B,\sum_{x_A\in\mathcal{X _A q(x_A|x_B)=1$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Moreover, we require $q(x_A|x_B) \equiv \sum_{x_C \in \mathcal{X _C q(x_A, x_C|x_B)$ and $q(x_A|x_B, x_C) \equiv q(x_A, x_C|x_B) / q(x_C|x_B)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: In other words, a probability kernel behaves almost like a conditional probability, except that it bypasses the marginal distribution on the indexing set $\mathcal{X _B$.}

elements.sent-2411-056: The measurement choice is denoted as a random variable, and the measurement outcome is described as a probability kernel over a fixed output alphabet given the measurement choice.

elements.sent-2411-061: The set of allowed state transformations $\mathcal{T}$ is formed by linear maps transforming a normalized state to a valid state.

elements.sent-2411-065: Among these assumptions, Assumptions 4 and 5, which correspond to how multiple systems compose into a joint one, shall be essential to our results.
elements.sent-2411-066: In particular, the no-signalling assumption (Assumption 5) captures the essence of relativity, where the light speed poses a fundamental limit to information transmission.

elements.sent-2411-071: Going beyond, one of the most well-studied GPTs is the ``box-world'' theory~\cite{popescu1994quantum,barrett2007information .}
elements.sent-2411-072: In brief, the box-world aims at generating the most nonlocal statistics in Bell tests subjected to relativity.
elements.sent-2411-073: In the simplest case, the Clauser-Horne-Shimony-Holt (CHSH) test~\cite{clauser1969proposed , consider two remote non-communicating observers, Alice and Bob.}

elements.section-2411-003: Nested Markov model in generalized probabilistic theories
elements.sent-2411-110: As an equality constraint, each conditional independence condition in a causal structure, or equivalently, a d-separation condition, reduces a dimension of the statistical manifold for the set of valid distributions.
elements.sent-2411-111: However, in the study of Bayesian networks, it is found that there are other equality constraints in general.
elements.sent-2411-112: Consider the causal structure of the mediation test in Fig.~\ref{fig:Verma (a) and assume $\Lambda$ is a classical random variable for now.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Using the Bayesian formula, one can prove the following expression \begin{equation}\label{eq:Verma} q(c|b)\equiv\sum_{A=a}p(a|x)p(c|x,a,b)\end{equation} is a function of only $B$ and $C$, independent of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-114: Such an equality is called the Verma constraint~\cite{robins1986new,verma1990equivalence .}
elements.sent-2411-115: Different from the usual conditional independence conditions between random variables, the Verma constraint cannot be identified through the d-separation statements in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-116: In classical causal theory, given a causal structure $G$, the set of probability distributions that satisfy all the usual conditional independence conditions and Verma constraints is considered as the nested Markov model associated with $G$~\cite{evans2017margins,richardson2023nested , which we denote as $\NEST(G)$.}
elements.sent-2411-117: The nested Markov model is proven to provide an algebraically complete description of the Bayesian network with latent variables, listing exactly all the equality constraints among observed variables~\cite{evans2017margins .}
elements.sent-2411-118: Namely, $\C(G)$ and $\NEST(G)$ define manifolds in the probability simplex that are equivalent up to the difference of inequality constraints.
elements.sent-2411-119: Since a classical causal structure usually poses additional constraints, we have $\C(G)\subseteq\NEST(G)$, and there are causal structures making the inclusion strict.
elements.sent-2411-120: The original derivation of Verma constraints is restricted to Bayesian networks~\cite{robins1986new,verma1990equivalence .}
elements.sent-2411-121: In this proof, besides the observed variables, the latent variables are also characterized as classical random variables.

elements.sent-2411-123: It is less clear whether Verma constraints and hence the nested Markov model remain valid for GPT causal theories.
elements.sent-2411-124: As suggested by the proven validity of d-separation in GPTs~\cite{henson2014theory , it is tempting to consider the Verma constraints as properties inherent to a causal structure, independent of the underlying physical theory.}
elements.sent-2411-125: Here, we confirm this statement positively.

elements.section-2411-004: A causal model between the nested Markov model and GPTs
elements.sent-2411-143: While the nested Markov model fully covers the predictions of GPTs, we shall show that the converse is not true.
elements.sent-2411-144: Namely, there exist causal structures such that $\GPT(G)\subsetneq\NEST(G)$.

elements.sent-2411-256: In this work, we study the physical interpretation of the nested Markov model, an algebraic approach to causality characterization.
elements.sent-2411-257: For this purpose, we look into GPTs, where latent variables may represent generalized physical states subjected to certain probabilistic rules and relativity principles.
elements.sent-2411-258: Given a causal structure, the nested Markov model is specified by equality constraints among observable variables, including the (conditional) independence conditions and Verma constraints.
elements.sent-2411-259: With the help of a graph-based definition of nested Markov models, we prove all these equality constraints are sound and complete regardless of the underlying physics.
elements.sent-2411-260: Consequently, GPT causal theories comply with the nested Markov model.
elements.sent-2411-261: On the contrary, we show causal structures in which the nested Markov model allows ``non-physical'' probability distributions among observable variables.
elements.sent-2411-262: To understand the gap between the nested Markov model and GPTs, we construct a new causal model, where every causal structure is considered a low-dimensional projection from a high-dimensional Bell-type structure.
elements.sent-2411-263: All the equality constraints originate from the projection of usual conditional independence conditions among relevant observable variables in the high-dimensional structure.
elements.sent-2411-264: The new model also predicts the emergence of inequality constraints from the projection of conditional independence conditions, strengthening the nested Markov model by further restricting the volume of statistical manifolds.
elements.sent-2411-265: However, GPTs still seem ``too strict'' compared to the new causal model; since as shown in the nonlocality swapping problem, causal structures with more than one latent variable, GPTs may pose additional inequality constraints due to the requirement of ``physical operations''.
elements.sent-2411-266: While we suggest the new model supersets any physically well-motivated causal theory, we anticipate an intermediate causal theory between the GPTs and the current model, which fully respects the causal structures and closes the gap between the algebraic and physical perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-267: One possibility is to consider hyper-graphs, adding not only observable variables but also latent ones.
elements.sent-2411-268: Some progress has been made in this direction~\cite{gisin2020constraints,wolfe2021quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-269: In a more radical attempt, as has been explored in some gravity theories, the underlying assumption of probabilities may be relaxed or even dropped~\cite{deutsch2015constructor,marletto2020witnessing .}
elements.sent-2411-270: Finally, inspired by GYNI and the projection technique, we discover a minimal causal structure separating Bayesian networks, GPTs, and the nested Markov model.
elements.sent-2411-271: While the new structure is a common substructure of larger ones, it is appealing to develop systematic criteria to determine whether a general causal structure manifests a gap between the predictions in various causal theories~\cite{henson2014theory .}
elements.sent-2411-272: In addition, we restrict our discussion to binary observable variables.
elements.sent-2411-273: We look forward to further study of the effect of variable alphabets on causal theories.

elements.sent-2411-282: We first make a few remarks on the GPT framework used in this work.
elements.sent-2411-283: First, the assumptions of convexity and no-signalling condition are important for causality discussions~\cite{chiribella2010probabilistic .}
elements.sent-2411-284: A vital fact is that these assumptions lead to the notion of a unique ``deterministic effect'', which prevents an event to affect previous ones that are well-defined in the spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-285: We shall show the exact definition of the deterministic effect later.
elements.sent-2411-286: A simple example is the ignorance of an event, which corresponds to a variable with a definite value that trivially indicates the existence of ``something''.
elements.sent-2411-287: Second, the GPTs subjected to assumptions in Box~\ref{box:GPTAssumption are considered ``complete GPTs''; namely, such a GPT first specifies the set of allowed states $\mathcal{S}$, including the single-party systems and multipartite systems.}
elements.sent-2411-288: The set of allowed transformations includes all the well-defined linear transformations, which maps a valid state that may be a part of a multipartite system to a valid state.
elements.sent-2411-289: This assumption is sometimes called the ``no-restriction rule''~\cite{chiribella2011information , as the set of valid physical operations is mathematically the largest.}
elements.sent-2411-290: In some results, the set of valid physical operations is further restricted by some physically motivated requirements~\cite{barnum2010entropy,chiribella2011information,janotta2013generalized .}
elements.sent-2411-291: In addition, a large variety of GPTs are also required to satisfy the local tomography requirement, where the global state of a multipartite system can be completely determined by specifying joint probabilities of outcomes for fiducial measurements performed simultaneously on each subsystem~\cite{barrett2007information .}
elements.sent-2411-292: A well-known GPT that fails to satisfy local tomography is the real-valued quantum theory~\cite{stueckelberg1960quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-293: Nevertheless, the no-restriction rule and the removal of local tomography only pose restrictions to the set of valid correlations within a causal structure.
elements.sent-2411-294: As we aim to minimize the gap between GPTs and algebraically motivated causal models, it suffices to consider complete GPTs.
elements.sent-2411-295: To see how the GPT framework works, we take the standard quantum theory as an example~\cite{sakurai2020modern .}
elements.sent-2411-296: A valid state in quantum theory is characterized as a density operator, which is a semi-definite positive operator with a unit trace.
elements.sent-2411-297: The state set comprises all the density operators, which is a convex set.
elements.sent-2411-298: Valid state transformations are characterized by completely positive and trace-preserving maps, or quantum channels, which also form a convex set.
elements.sent-2411-299: Quantum measurements, which are special quantum channels, can be generally described as positive operator-valued measures (POVMs).
elements.sent-2411-300: When measuring a quantum state $\rho$ with a POVM $\{M_i\}_i$, where the POVM elements satisfy $M_i\geq 0,\sum_i M_i=I$, the measurement outcome $i$ is obtained with probability $p_i=\tr(\rho M_i)$.
elements.sent-2411-301: The zero state can be described by an event that does not occur in the measurement.
elements.sent-2411-302: For a single-party state, by applying an information-complete state tomography, such as the measurements formed by the eigenvectors of Pauli operators $\sigma_x,\sigma_y,\sigma_z$ in the qubit case, the state can be fully characterized via the measurement outcome probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-303: The fiducial measurements thus correspond to the quantum measurements for tomography.

elements.sent-2411-561: Consider the graph in Fig.~\ref{fig:Triangle (a) with all the observed vertices representing binary random variables, and a probability distribution $p(A,B,C,X,Z)$, where \begin{equation}\begin{split} \sum_{a}p(a,b,c|x,z) \equiv p(b,c|z), \forall b,c,z\in\{0,1\}, \sum_{b}p(a,b,c|x,z) \equiv p(a|x)p(c|z), \forall a,c,x,z\in\{0,1\}, \sum_{c}p(a,b,c|x,z) \equiv p(a,b|x), \forall a,b,x\in\{0,1\}, p(a,b=0,c|x,z) =p(b=0)\frac{1}{2}\delta_{a\oplus c=xz}. \end{split}\end{equation} The first three equations guarantee that the probability distribution satisfies all the non-signalling constraints, hence $p\in\mathcal{I (G)$.}}
elements.formula-2411-041: \begin{equation} \sum_{a}p(a,b,c|x,z) \equiv p(b,c|z), \forall b,c,z\in\{0,1\}, \sum_{b}p(a,b,c|x,z) \equiv p(a|x)p(c|z), \forall a,c,x,z\in\{0,1\}, \sum_{c}p(a,b,c|x,z) \equiv p(a,b|x), \forall a,b,x\in\{0,1\}, p(a,b=0,c|x,z) =p(b=0)\frac{1}{2}\delta_{a\oplus c=xz}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-562: In addition, we have $\PSS(G)=\IND(G)$ by construction.
elements.sent-2411-563: Suppose there is a GPT $\mathcal{G}$ that can generate the probability distribution $p$.
elements.sent-2411-564: Then, the bipartite non-signalling probability distribution $q(A,C|X,Z)$ with \begin{equation}q(a,c|x,z)=\frac{1}{2}\delta_{a\oplus c=xz}\end{equation} belongs to the set of valid states $\mathcal{S}$ in this theory.
elements.sent-2411-565: This probability distribution is also called the standard Popescu-Rohrlich (PR) box~\cite{popescu1994quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-566: In the GPT $\mathcal{G}$, the most general operations that act on this bipartite state allows the state to be transformed into a probability distribution~\cite{barrett2005nonlocal \begin{equation}\begin{split} q'(a,c|x,z) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}, if a\oplus c=xz\oplus\alpha x\oplus\beta z\oplus \gamma, 0, otherwise, \end{cases} \end{split}\end{equation} where $\alpha,\beta,\gamma=0,1$.}

elements.sent-2411-567: We term these probabilities an $(\alpha,\beta,\gamma)$-PR box, where the standard PR box corresponds to the case with $\alpha=\beta=\gamma=0$.
elements.sent-2411-568: In addition, consider the set of allowed states on a single-party system.
elements.sent-2411-569: According to the convexity assumption for a valid GPT, any fixed input-output distribution should belong to the set of valid states.
elements.sent-2411-570: According to the parallel composition assumption, the tensor product of two such probabilities for a bipartite system represents a valid state.
elements.sent-2411-571: There are sixteen inequivalent states of such, which we denote as $p_{L_i ,i=0,\cdots,15$.}
elements.sent-2411-572: By slightly modifying the results in~\citet{short2006entanglement , we can prove that for $\Lambda_1$ and $\Lambda_2$ to be either an $(\alpha,\beta,\gamma)$-PR box or a local distribution $p_{L_i}$, the model cannot generate the probability distribution $p$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:PRBox .}}
elements.sent-2411-573: Using the results in~\citet{bierhorst2016geometric and the convexity assumption, any valid bipartite GPT state in $\mathcal{G}$ with binary-valued observables can be decomposed into a convex combination of one $(\alpha,\beta,\gamma)$-PR box and several $p_{L_i}$.}
elements.sent-2411-574: Then, by using a convexity argument, which is consistent with the convexity assumption of the GPT, we arrive at the conclusion that $\mathcal{G}$ cannot generate the probability distribution $p$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:PRBox .}
elements.section-2411-012: Common separation between classical and GPT causal models
elements.sent-2411-575: In this section, we prove that Eq.~\eqref{eq:GYNIproj1 does not admit a causal explanation in a classical Bayesian network.}
elements.sent-2411-576: To prove this result, first note that the set of probability distributions that can be generated from the causal DAG $G$ of Fig.~\ref{fig:GYNDAG (a) within a GPT is convex, as there is only one latent variable.}
elements.sent-2411-577: In particular, the set of distributions $\C(G)$ within a Bayesian network is a convex polytope.
elements.sent-2411-578: Therefore, we can list all extremal points of $\C(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-579: For this purpose, we take advantage of the projection technique in Def.~\ref{def:projection .}
elements.sent-2411-580: In the tripartite Bell test $H(G)$ of Fig.~\ref{fig:GYNDAG (b), when all the observed variables are binary, the set of probability distributions $\C(H(G))$ is spanned by the following extremal points: \begin{equation}p(a,b,c|x,y,z)=\begin{cases} 1, a=\alpha x\oplus\beta,b=\gamma y\oplus\delta, c=\theta z+\eta, 0, \text{otherwise}, \end{cases}\end{equation} where $\alpha,\beta,\gamma,\delta,\theta,\eta \in \{0,1\}$.}
elements.formula-2411-044: \begin{equation}p(a,b,c|x,y,z)= 1, a=\alpha x\oplus\beta,b=\gamma y\oplus\delta, c=\theta z+\eta, 0, otherwise, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-581: In total, there are $2^6=64$ extreme points in $\C(H(G))$.
elements.sent-2411-582: Next, we take the projection and obtain the extremal points in $\C(G)$ by taking $Y=A,Z=B$.
elements.sent-2411-583: Note that the projection results in some degeneracy.
elements.sent-2411-584: After the projection, the values of $A=a,B=b$, and $C=c$ are determined by \begin{equation}\begin{split} a =\alpha x\oplus\beta, b =\gamma a\oplus\delta=\gamma (\alpha x\oplus\beta)\oplus\delta, c =\theta z+\eta=\theta (\gamma (\alpha x\oplus\beta)\oplus\delta)+\eta. \end{split}\end{equation} We list the probability distributions in detail with respect to the values of $a$.
elements.formula-2411-045: \begin{equation} a =\alpha x\oplus\beta, b =\gamma a\oplus\delta=\gamma (\alpha x\oplus\beta)\oplus\delta, c =\theta z+\eta=\theta (\gamma (\alpha x\oplus\beta)\oplus\delta)+\eta. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-585: Case 1: When $\alpha=\beta=0,b=\delta,c=\theta\delta\oplus\eta$, there is a deterministic output of $A=0$.
elements.sent-2411-586: There are four extremal points in this case.
elements.sent-2411-587: We list the conditional probability distributions of $p(a,b,c|x)$ in Table~\ref{table:case1 .}
elements.sent-2411-588: A blank term in the table means that the conditional probability is zero.
elements.sent-2411-589: Case 2: When $\alpha=0,\beta=1,b=\gamma\oplus\delta,c=\theta(\gamma\oplus\delta)\oplus\eta$, there is a deterministic output of $A=1$.
elements.sent-2411-590: We list the conditional probability distributions of $p(a,b,c|x)$ in Table~\ref{table:case2 .}
elements.sent-2411-591: Case 3: When $\alpha=1,\beta=0$, there is a deterministic output of $A=X$.. Consequently, $b=\gamma X\oplus\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-592: There are twelve extremal points in this case.
elements.sent-2411-593: We list the conditional probability distributions of $p(a,b,c|x)$ in Table~\ref{table:case3 .. Case 4: $\alpha=\beta=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-594: There are twelve extremal points in this case, which coincide with the ones in Case 3 up to a flip.
elements.sent-2411-595: We list the conditional probability distributions of $p(a,b,c|x)$ in Table~\ref{table:case4 .}
elements.sent-2411-596: Afterwards, we can set up a linear programming optimization to test whether Eq.~\eqref{eq:GYNIproj1 can be written as a convex combination of these extremal points.}
elements.sent-2411-597: It can be easily checked that this is not possible, hence proving that the probability distribution in Eq.~\eqref{eq:GYNIproj1 is outside $\C(G)$.}
